name: Winter
description: Snow, cold weather, holidays, and winter facts

questions.q: On what date does winter typically begin in the Northern Hemisphere?

source.notes: Winter solstice. Shortest day of the year. Exact date varies by year due to leap cycles.

questions.q: What is the basic geometric shape of all natural snowflakes?

source.notes: Six-sided symmetry comes from water molecule crystal structure.

questions.q: Where was the coldest temperature ever recorded on Earth (−89.2°C / −128.6°F)?

source.notes: Recorded July 21, 1983. Soviet research base on the Antarctic Plateau.

questions.q: What is the Northern Hemisphere's shortest day of the year also known as?

source.notes: 'Solstice' from Latin 'sol' (sun) + 'sistere' (to stop). Sun appears to stop moving.

questions.q: Which months are winter in the Northern Hemisphere?

source.notes: Astronomical winter is Dec 21 - Mar 20. Meteorological winter is Dec 1 - Feb 28/29.

questions.q: When many animals sleep through winter, this is called what?

source.notes: Bears, ground squirrels, bats, and groundhogs hibernate. Body temperature drops significantly.

questions.q: Most Winter Olympic sports take place on which surfaces?

source.notes: First Winter Olympics: 1924 in Chamonix, France.

questions.q: What is the name of the annual sled-dog race held every March in Alaska?

source.notes: Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race. ~1,000 miles from Anchorage to Nome. Runs ~9 days.

questions.q: What is 'frazil' in wintry contexts?
choices: Ice crystals forming in flowing water

source.notes: Small, disc-shaped ice crystals that form on the surface of super-cooled running water.

questions.q: What is the phenomenon called when the Sun appears to 'stand still' at the solstices?

source.notes: The Sun reaches its lowest or highest point in the sky at noon.

questions.q: What name is given to the cold, dry winds that blow from Siberia across East Asia?

source.notes: Large atmospheric high-pressure system. Brings extreme cold to Mongolia, northern China, Japan.

questions.q: What is the common name for the phenomenon where your breath visibly condenses in cold air?

source.notes: Warm, moist exhaled breath condenses when it hits cold air.

questions.q: What type of cloud produces snowfall?

source.notes: Also cumulonimbus in heavy snow events. Need sub-freezing temperatures throughout cloud.

questions.q: On average, how much water does 12 cm (~5 inches) of fresh snow melt down to?

source.notes: Common ratio: 10-12 inches of snow melts to ~1 inch of water (10-12:1). Wet snow is denser.

questions.q: What winter phenomenon occurs when cold rain freezes on contact with surfaces, creating a dangerous glaze?

source.notes: Freezing rain. Differs from sleet (already frozen) and hail (convective origin).

questions.q: When does winter begin in the Southern Hemisphere?

source.notes: June 20-22. Southern summer is December-February.

questions.q: Which citrus fruits are traditionally at peak season in winter in North America?

source.notes: Also mandarins, grapefruits, lemons. Florida and California peak crops.

questions.q: What is the bright natural light display visible at high latitudes in winter, often called the 'northern lights'?

source.notes: Caused by solar particles interacting with the atmosphere. Aurora australis is the southern version.

questions.q: Groundhog Day, where a groundhog's shadow predicts six more weeks of winter, is celebrated on what date?

source.notes: Punxsutawney Phil is the most famous groundhog. Derived from German Candlemas traditions.

questions.q: What is the approximate freezing point of pure water in Fahrenheit?

source.notes: 0°C = 32°F = 273.15 K. Salt water freezes lower (around 28°F for ocean water).
